articles.headline: Regeneron wins landmark FDA approval for first gene therapy treating hearing loss
articles.summary: The FDA approved Regeneron's Otarmeni, the first-ever gene therapy for genetic hearing loss, using a dual AAV vector to treat children and adults with OTOF gene mutations. The approval came through an accelerated 61-day review under the National Priority Voucher program, with clinical trial data showing 80% of patients experienced improved hearing. This marks the first disease-modifying treatment for OTOF-related deafness, which affects 2-8% of inherited hearing loss cases.
articles.why_it_matters: This landmark approval validates Regeneron's gene therapy platform in a new therapeutic area and could signal the beginning of a broader sensory restoration market, with potential applications beyond hearing loss.

articles.headline: Getting NIH funds is more competitive — and confusing — than ever
articles.summary: NIH grant success rates have plummeted to just 13% under new budgeting strategies and policy changes, forcing academic researchers to spend more time on grant applications while many labs face funding crises. A STAT survey of nearly 1,000 researchers found 45% experienced grant delays and 43% cancelled research projects, with the agency spending 30% less than normal at this point in the fiscal year.
articles.why_it_matters: Severe NIH funding constraints could reshape the biomedical innovation landscape, potentially driving more academic talent toward industry partnerships and affecting the pipeline of early-stage research that feeds into drug development.
